title: Rheumatic heart disease in Africa: two cents, two trials, two hundred and forty thousand deaths per year
body: How does Africa rheumatic heart disease burden correspond to registered trial activity for prevention? We queried ClinicalTrials.gov for interventional trials matching rheumatic heart disease, rheumatic fever, or penicillin prophylaxis across eight African countries, the United States, India, and the United Kingdom, with subgroup searches for streptococcal pharyngitis. Africa had approximately 2 registered RHD trials compared with 23 in the United States, despite carrying 78 percent of global RHD mortality and an estimated 240,000 deaths annually. Benzathine penicillin G costs two US cents per dose and is on the WHO Essential Medicines List, yet chronic global shortages leave millions of African children unprotected. High-income countries eliminated RHD decades ago using universal penicillin treatment for streptococcal pharyngitis, proving the gap reflects access rather than scientific uncertainty. Uganda, Ethiopia, and Mozambique had the highest burden rates yet zero or one trial each. This analysis is limited to one registry and cannot account for burden estimate uncertainty in low-resource settings.

sentences.text: How does Africa's rheumatic heart disease burden correspond to its share of registered clinical trial activity for prevention and treatment?

We queried ClinicalTrials.gov API v2 for interventional trials matching rheumatic heart disease, rheumatic fever, or penicillin prophylaxis across eight African countries, the United States, India, Brazil, and the United Kingdom, with subgroup searches for streptococcal pharyngitis and benzathine penicillin.
Africa had approximately 2 registered RHD trials compared with 23 in the United States, despite carrying 78 percent of global RHD mortality and an estimated 240,000 deaths annually.

Benzathine penicillin G costs two US cents per dose and is on the WHO Essential Medicines List, yet chronic shortages and absent delivery infrastructure leave millions of African children unprotected.

High-income countries eliminated RHD decades ago using universal penicillin treatment for streptococcal pharyngitis, proving the intervention works and the gap reflects access and political priority rather than scientific uncertainty.
Uganda, Ethiopia, and Mozambique carried the highest RHD burden rates yet had zero or one registered trial each across all related categories.
This analysis is limited to a single English-language registry and cannot account for the wide uncertainty intervals around RHD burden estimates in low-resource settings.

outsideNote.data: ClinicalTrials.gov API v2, 8 African countries, 4 comparators, GBD 2019 burden data

ai_transparency: LLM assistance was used for drafting and language editing. The author reviewed and edited the manuscript and takes responsibility for the final content.
